Title: Homeostasis of flavonoids and triterpenoids most likely modulates starch metabolism for pollen tube penetration in rice

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in root, culm, leaf, inflorescence, pistil/stigma and anther; preferentially in root stele, stem, leaf and pistil; also in young panicles and young anthers; very low in mature anther and near background in pollen grains; GFP not detected in mature pollen tubes
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in root stele, stem, leaf, pistil, young panicle and young anther; low in mature anther; background in pollen grains
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Type III polyketide synthase; chalcone synthase family
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Entry/committed step of flavonoid pathway leading to chalcones, flavonols and related flavonoids
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses 4-coumaroyl-CoA with malonyl-CoA units to form naringenin chalcone, the first committed intermediate of flavonoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies the core precursor pool for flavonoid/flavonol biosynthesis and indirectly maintains starch hydrolysis and energy metabolism by preserving flavonoid–triterpenoid metabolic homeostasis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Flavonoids/flavonols; indirectly starch-derived monosaccharides and ATP status affecting pollen tube penetration
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports rice male fertility by enabling pollen tube penetration into stigma tissue through metabolic homeostasis linked to starch hydrolysis, glycometabolism, ATP production and turgor
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of OsCHS1 abolishes flavonoid biosynthesis, causes triterpenoid accumulation, reduces α-amylase activity, impairs starch hydrolysis and glycometabolism, lowers ATP and turgor, and blocks pollen tube penetration into stigma
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OsCHS1 was identified by mapping, RNA-seq prioritization, complementation and CRISPR as the causal gene of the male-sterile xt6 phenotype. 2. OsCHS1 mutation eliminated detectable flavonols in pollen, pollen tubes and pistils and strongly reduced pollen flavonoid metabolites. 3. Loss of OsCHS1 disrupted flavonoid/triterpenoid homeostasis, with increased triterpenoids and precursor phenolics. 4. Mutant pollen showed reduced α-amylase activity, slower amyloplast hydrolysis, lower monosaccharides, weakened TCA-cycle output, reduced ATP/ADP ratio and decreased turgor. 5. These metabolic defects prevented pollen tube penetration in vivo but not in vitro germination or tube elongation.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as the first key/committed enzyme of flavonoid biosynthesis; a decisive upstream control point for the observed metabolic phenotype
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In vivo pollination/pollen tube penetration in stigma; exogenous quercetin+kaempferol, glucose or ATP treatments failed to rescue; natural field growth conditions
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native OsCHS1 promoter in complementation, GUS and OsCHS1-GFP constructs
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Null mutants show near-zero seed set due to male sterility; no obvious vegetative defects reported
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Map-based cloning; resequencing; RNA-seq; complementation; CRISPR/Cas9 knockout; GUS/qRT-PCR expression analysis; subcellular localization; DPBA staining; LC-MS flavonoid metabolomics; α-amylase assay; sugar metabolomics; ATP/ADP and turgor measurements; TEM/SEM
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq of WT stigmas pollinated with WT vs xt6 pollen (accession NA); flavonoid metabolomics source data Data S1; sugar metabolomics source data Data S2
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for creating male-sterile lines in rice breeding; should be preserved for fertility, while loss-of-function can serve as a sterility resource; engineering must consider severe reproductive penalty
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Exogenous quercetin and kaempferol, unlike in maize/petunia, did not rescue rice mutant fertility, indicating a distinct mechanism. The paper infers OsCHS1 acts indirectly on starch metabolism via metabolite homeostasis rather than directly on α-amylase genes or ROS.
